content.text: What it is
Trace sampling strategy for agent debugging

is the approach of selecting different sampling rates and policies depending on the operational context — development, production-normal, active incident, or post-incident forensic investigation. A single static sampling rate cannot serve all contexts: 100 % sampling is cost-prohibitive in production at scale, but a 2 % probabilistic rate is useless when you are trying to reproduce a specific error during an incident. A sampling strategy defines rules for each context and provides a control mechanism for operators to adjust rates without redeploying code.
Why it matters

Debugging agentic AI failures is fundamentally different from debugging typical services. A failing agent may produce an error on iteration 8 of a 12-step reasoning loop, with the root cause buried in a tool call at iteration 3. If that specific chain of spans was not sampled, the debug data simply does not exist. Conversely, during normal production operation, sampling 100 % of traces is economically infeasible and generates so much data that signal is lost in noise. A context-aware sampling strategy solves both problems: high rates when you need every trace, low rates when you need cost efficiency, with smooth transitions between modes that do not require deployment.
Benefits
Captures complete traces during incidents without permanently inflating storage costs.
Developers get full trace coverage in staging, enabling accurate debugging before production deployment.
Automatic TTL on incident sampling overrides prevents engineers from forgetting to revert to normal rates after the incident.
Service-scoped overrides allow targeted sampling increase for the affected service without inflating costs for unrelated services.
Dynamic configuration API eliminates the need for a code deploy to change sampling rates during a time-critical incident.
Trade-offs and limitations
A dynamic sampling override API is itself a security surface — an attacker who can call it could flood the trace backend with data or disable sampling entirely.
Rate changes do not apply retroactively — traces that occurred before the override are gone if they were under the previous sampling rate.
High incident sampling rates can overwhelm the trace backend if the affected service generates extreme volume — requires backend auto-scaling.
Context detection ("is this an incident?") requires integration with your incident management system; manual invocation adds latency.
Frequent emergency override activations may indicate that the baseline tail sampling policy is insufficient and needs tuning.
How it works
The sampling strategy has three tiers. In
development and staging
, 100 % of spans are exported to a local or team-specific Jaeger or Tempo instance with no tail sampling. In
production (normal)
, tail sampling applies: 100 % of error traces, 100 % of slow traces (P99 > threshold), and a 2 % probabilistic baseline. During an
active incident

, the incident commander triggers a sampling override for the affected service via a control plane API. The OTel Collector reads the override config (served as a ConfigMap or remote config endpoint) and raises the probabilistic rate to 50 or 100 % for that service, with an automatic 2-hour TTL.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Sampling strategy selector diagram: development gets 100%, production gets tail sampling, active incidents get high-rate override with TTL

content.text: The strategy is chosen based on deployment context, not a single global rate. Development environments sample everything. Production uses tail sampling with outcome-based policies. When an incident is declared, the IC applies a time-limited override for the specific affected service, automatically reverting after the TTL.
When to use it
Any production agent system where a single sampling rate is insufficient for both normal operation and incident debugging.
When incident post-mortems repeatedly cite lack of trace data as a factor in extended MTTR.
When developer feedback indicates that trace coverage in staging is insufficient to reproduce production bugs.
When observability costs are significant enough that a static high sample rate is not acceptable.
How to implement

Configure the OTel SDK to read deployment environment from an environment variable and set the head-sampling rate to 1.0 in development, 0.0 in production (all tail-sampling decisions made at collector).
Configure the OTel Collector tail_sampling processor for production: error policy (100%), latency policy (100%), probabilistic baseline (2%).
Implement a sampling override ConfigMap (Kubernetes) or remote config endpoint that the collector polls every 30 seconds.
Build an incident override CLI or API endpoint that writes the override to the ConfigMap and sets a TTL. Integrate with your incident management system's webhook.
Implement automatic TTL enforcement: a controller watches the override ConfigMap and deletes expired entries, triggering the collector to revert to the default policy.
Add Prometheus alerts for high incident sampling rates still active after the incident resolution time to catch forgotten overrides.
Examples

# Kubernetes ConfigMap: sampling override (set during incident)
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: sampling-overrides
  namespace: observability
data:
  overrides.yaml: |
    # Active overrides (IC-managed during incidents)
    overrides:
      - service_name: customer-support-agent
        team: customer-experience
        probabilistic_rate: 1.0    # 100% during incident
        reason: "INC-2847: elevated error rate in session start"
        expires_at: "2026-07-14T10:30:00Z"
        ic_user: "alice@company.com"
    # Default production policy (used when no override)
    default_policy:
      error_rate: 1.0
      latency_threshold_ms: 5000
      latency_rate: 1.0
      baseline_rate: 0.02

# Incident sampling override CLI
import argparse
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from kubernetes import client, config
import yaml

def set_sampling_override(
    service_name: str,
    rate: float,
    reason: str,
    ic_user: str,
    ttl_minutes: int = 120,
):
    config.load_incluster_config()
    v1 = client.CoreV1Api()
    
    expires_at = (datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(minutes=ttl_minutes)).isoformat()
    
    override = {
        "service_name": service_name,
        "probabilistic_rate": rate,
        "reason": reason,
        "expires_at": expires_at,
        "ic_user": ic_user,
    }
    
    cm = v1.read_namespaced_config_map("sampling-overrides", "observability")
    overrides_data = yaml.safe_load(cm.data["overrides.yaml"])
    
    # Remove any existing override for this service
    overrides_data["overrides"] = [
        o for o in overrides_data.get("overrides", [])
        if o["service_name"] != service_name
    ]
    overrides_data["overrides"].append(override)
    
    cm.data["overrides.yaml"] = yaml.dump(overrides_data)
    v1.replace_namespaced_config_map("sampling-overrides", "observability", cm)
    print(f"Override set for {service_name}: {rate*100:.0f}% until {expires_at}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--service", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--rate", type=float, default=1.0)
    parser.add_argument("--reason", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--ttl-minutes", type=int, default=120)
    args = parser.parse_args()
    set_sampling_override(args.service, args.rate, args.reason, ttl_minutes=args.ttl_minutes, ic_user="cli")

# OTel Collector: read sampling overrides from ConfigMap
extensions:
  file_storage/sampling_overrides:
    directory: /etc/otel/sampling

processors:
  tail_sampling:
    decision_wait: 30s
    num_traces: 500000
    # Load override config from mounted ConfigMap
    policies:
      # Incident overrides (dynamically loaded)
      - name: incident-override-customer-support-agent
        type: probabilistic
        probabilistic:
          sampling_percentage: 100  # overridden by CM controller

      # Default policies (always active)
      - name: errors-always
        type: status_code
        status_code: {status_codes: [ERROR]}

      - name: slow-traces
        type: latency
        latency: {threshold_ms: 5000}

      - name: baseline
        type: probabilistic
        probabilistic: {sampling_percentage: 2}
Real-world example
A customer service AI platform experiences elevated error rates (8 % vs. normal 0.5 %) on its scheduling agent. The IC declares an incident and triggers the sampling override CLI:
obs-override --service scheduling-agent --rate 1.0 --reason "INC-3012" --ttl-minutes 120

. Within 45 seconds (the collector polling interval), the scheduling agent is sampled at 100 %. The IC queries Grafana and immediately sees the full trace structure: an error at step 4 of the booking flow caused by a timezone conversion bug, visible in the tool call output attribute. Without the override, only 2 % of traces would have been retained, statistically showing roughly one trace per minute — sufficient to see the error exists, but insufficient to trace the call path. The incident is resolved in 22 minutes; at T+2h, the override automatically reverts.
Common mistakes
Forgetting to revert incident overrides after the incident, causing permanent high-rate sampling and unexpected storage costs.
Setting the TTL too short (30 minutes) — the incident may require continued sampling during resolution and post-incident verification.
Applying a global sampling override instead of a service-scoped one, inflating costs across all services during a localised incident.
Not having the override tool accessible during incidents — if it requires VPN access that is degraded, the IC cannot trigger it.
Head-sampling in production (making the decision at the SDK level) instead of tail-sampling — once spans are dropped at the SDK, they cannot be recovered.
Best practices
Always use tail sampling in production — keep all spans in the buffer long enough to make outcome-aware decisions, never drop at the SDK.
Default TTL of 2 hours is appropriate for most incidents; allow IC to extend in 1-hour increments, not indefinitely.
Integrate the sampling override CLI into your incident runbook so ICs invoke it as a first response, not an afterthought.
Monitor the trace storage ingestion rate and alert if it exceeds 2x normal volume — an unrecognised override or traffic anomaly needs investigation.
Test the override mechanism quarterly in a staging environment — confirm that the config change propagates to the collector within 2 collector poll cycles.
Performance and scalability

A 100 % sampling override on a high-volume service can produce 50–100x the normal trace volume for that service. The trace backend must be able to absorb this burst. Configure Tempo with burst-capable ingest (S3-backed compactor handles backlog), and alert if the ingest lag exceeds 5 minutes. The OTel Collector tail sampling buffer must be sized for the peak volume during overrides — under-sizing causes the buffer to evict traces before the wait window expires.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
OTel Collector tail_sampling
Core sampling engine
ConfigMap-reloaded policy
Kubernetes ConfigMap
Dynamic override config store
Hot-reloaded by collector
Grafana Tempo
Trace backend for incident data
S3-backed, handles burst ingest
Honeycomb Refinery
Managed tail sampling with API
SaaS option; dynamic rules via API

Context-aware + override

Security and reliability

The sampling override API is a potential attack vector: an attacker who can set sampling to 0 % can blind the observability system. Restrict write access to the sampling override ConfigMap to the incident commander role only, enforced by Kubernetes RBAC. Log all override changes to the audit trail. Rate-limit the override API to prevent rapid oscillation that could degrade the collector.
Key takeaways
No single sampling rate serves all contexts — use a strategy that differentiates development, normal production, and active incidents.
Always use tail sampling in production; head sampling drops errors and anomalies disproportionately.
A dynamic override mechanism that does not require a code deploy is essential for incident response — time to full trace visibility must be under 2 minutes.
Scope overrides to the affected service; a global override inflates cost across all services.
TTL enforcement is not optional — forgotten overrides compound into sustained cost overruns.
Integrate the override CLI into incident runbooks so it is a reflex, not a discussion point during a live incident.
